heading: capitolo VI, tutto dedicato alla Trinità, nell'intento di trovarne tracce nell'Antico Testamento, Pietro Alfonsi presenta, accanto a un'analisi del Nome e delle sue lettere componenti277 anche un fortunato
card_summary: This chunk is Campanini's editorial commentary tracing Zorzi's sources for his analysis of the Tetragrammaton. Campanini demonstrates that Zorzi's mystical etymologizing of the Hebrew letters (yod, he, vav, he) derives from a chain of medieval anti-Jewish polemicists — Pietro Alfonsi, Pietro di Blois, Raimondo Martini (Pugio fidei) — and is mediated directly through Pietro Galatino's De arcanis catholicae veritatis (1518) and Johannes Reuchlin's De arte cabalistica. The one apparent originality Zorzi retains — interpreting yod as 'scientia' rather than 'principium,' and the numerological circularity of the Tetragrammaton's letters — is also found in Reuchlin and can be traced back to Pico della Mirandola's Conclusio 68. Campanini further shows that Zorzi elsewhere deploys far richer kabbalistic material (gematria on tal/rugiada and the milluy technique) that he deliberately suppresses in this passage, suggesting a considered choice to avoid explicitly Trinitarian polemics.
essay:
## Overview

Pages 62–66 of Campanini's edition consist almost entirely of his editorial apparatus — a dense footnote or interstitial commentary tracing the genealogy of Zorzi's treatment of the Tetragrammaton. The chunk reveals how Zorzi's second argumentative line (concerning the divine Name as archetype) dissolves, when examined source by source, into a succession of earlier authors. Campanini's analysis is both a source-critical exercise and a meditation on what, if anything, remains genuinely original in Zorzi.

## The Diagrammatic Tradition: Pietro Alfonsi and Joachim of Fiore

Campanini opens by noting that Pietro Alfonsi, in a chapter of his anti-Jewish polemic dedicated to the Trinity, presents alongside a letter-analysis of the Tetragrammaton a diagramma:

> formato da tre cerchi, ciascuno dei quali comprende due lettere del Nome Tetragramma, inclusi in un quarto cerchio che ricomprende e delimita l'intera figura.

<cite>p. 62</cite>

This diagram — three circles each containing two letters of the four-letter Name, enclosed in a larger fourth circle — reappears in Joachim of Fiore's *Commento all'Apocalisse*, and Campanini does not exclude its presence behind the famous rose-of-light passage in *Paradiso* XXXIII, 115–120. Crucially, while Zorzi mentions three letters in the Tetragrammaton, Campanini observes that he conspicuously refrains from exploiting the Trinitarian anti-Jewish polemical dividend this tradition offered: Zorzi "si astiene dal cogliere i frutti polemici anti-giudaici dell'implicita conferma della dottrina della Trinità."

## The Chain of Medieval Anti-Jewish Polemic

The same Trinitarian reading of the Tetragrammaton (*unum et trinum*) appears in Pietro di Blois's *Contra perfidiam Judaeorum*, and more fully in the most important medieval anti-Jewish polemicist, Raimondo Martini (the Catalan Ramón Martí), author of the *Pugio fidei*. Campanini shows that Martini was the first to gloss the Hebrew letter *he* as the initial of *hawayah* (rendered by Martini as *essentia*, by Zorzi more correctly as *esse*). The transmission path is confirmed by circumstantial evidence: Agostino Giustiniani, bishop of Nebbio, had edited Porchetto Salvago's *Victoria Porcheti adversus impios Hebraeos* (Paris 1520), which reproduces the *Pugio fidei*'s arguments near-verbatim. Campanini characterizes this as going "ben oltre il limite del plagio."

## Pietro Galatino as Immediate Source

The key immediate source for Zorzi, Campanini argues, is the Franciscan Pietro Colonna (Galatino), *De arcanis catholicae veritatis* (Ortona a Mare, Gershom Soncino, 1518):

> Una fonte dopo l'altra l'intero contenuto della seconda linea argomentativa di Zorzi pare sciogliersi, lasciandosi ridurre ad autori più o meno noti che, con intenti assai diversi, lo avevano preceduto. Persino il richiamo al versetto di Giovanni (1,2-3), citato con frequenza da Zorzi, si trova già in Galatino.

<cite>p. 63</cite>

Galatino himself drew on Pietro Alfonsi and especially on the *Pugio fidei* (which he avoids naming). The only residue Campanini can identify as Zorzi's apparent contribution is: (1) interpreting *yod* as 'scientia' rather than Galatino's 'principium'; and (2) a numerological observation on the 'circularity' of the numbers corresponding to the Tetragrammaton's letters.

## Reuchlin, Pico, and the Spherical Numbers

Even these residues, Campanini shows, are traceable. The question of *numeri circolari* or *sferici* goes back to Pico della Mirandola's *Conclusio 68 secundum opinionem propriam*:

> Qui sciverit quid sit denarius in arithmetica formali, et cognoverit naturam primi numeri spherici, sciet illud quod ego adhuc apud aliquem cabalistam non legi, et est, quod sit fundamentum secreti magni Iobelei in Cabala.

<cite>p. 64</cite>

This deliberately enigmatic Pichian thesis prompted Christian kabbalists to find a solution. Campanini notes the mathematical background: the identity between circular and spherical numbers had been established by the Neoplatonic mathematician Theon of Smyrna in his *Expositio rerum mathematicarum ad legendum Platonem utilium*, translated (but not published) by Marsilio Ficino. For Zorzi, however, the traditional definition sufficed — as attested by Cassiodorus and Isidore of Seville. Reuchlin, in the third book of *De arte cabalistica*, explicitly cited Pico and resolved the problem by using a *sphaera plana* (the projection of a sphere onto a plane), thereby paving the road for Zorzi's commentary.

## The yod as 'Scientia': Another Reuchlin Source

For the interpretation of *yod* as 'scientia', Campanini points to a passage in Reuchlin's third book:

> "La decima lettera, yod, significa l'essenza delle intelligenze della decima emanazione. (…) La potenza di Dio le illumina mediante il nono coro [angelico] ed esse infondono ai figli degli uomini la conoscenza, la scienza delle cose e l'attività produttrice di miracoli."

<cite>p. 65</cite>

Campanini allows that this is not entirely determinative and that Zorzi may have been working from the straightforward Hebrew etymology (*yedi'ah* = scientia), but the Reuchlin parallel is suggestive.

## Zorzi's Richer Kabbalistic Knowledge Elsewhere

Campanini then demonstrates that Zorzi possessed far richer kabbalistic resources than he chose to deploy here. In the seventh tone of the first canticle of *De harmonia mundi*, discussing the symbolic meaning of dew (*tal/rugiada*), Zorzi writes:

> La parola di Dio, inoltre, è il canale, la teca e il fodero della divinità, come טל, tal, che significa rugiada, è il fodero del santissimo Nome di quattro lettere, per rivelare un elemento dei segreti della teologia esoterica degli ebrei.

<cite>p. 65–66</cite>

Campanini unpacks this as a sophisticated gematria: the great Name of God (Exodus 3:14, *ehyeh asher ehyeh*) yields by isopsephia a value of 21, equivalent to three-quarters of the Tetragrammaton (yod-he-vav). The *milluy de alfin* technique (filling the name by calculating the numerical value of the spelled-out letter names) produces 39 (kaf=20, aleph-he=6, yod-vav-dalet=13), matching the value of *tal* (dew). This demonstrates that Zorzi "disponeva di migliori informazioni, di provenienza autenticamente cabbalistica, se solo avesse voluto impiegarle in questo contesto."

## Zorzi's Prudence and Aristotle via Proclus

The chunk closes with a note that Zorzi, after adducing Aristotle's scholastic axiom — that nothing can give what it does not have — reads it through Proclus. This gesture of interpreting a commonplace Aristotelian principle through a Neoplatonic lens is characteristic of Zorzi's method throughout *De harmonia mundi*.

## Significance for Zorzi's Larger Project

This chunk reveals the fundamental tension in Zorzi's kabbalistic project: he inherits a tradition shaped by anti-Jewish conversion polemic (Martini, Alfonsi, Galatino) but deliberately strips it of its polemical weaponry, declining to read the Tetragrammaton explicitly as confirmation of the Trinity or as a Messianic proof-text. Campanini's source archaeology shows that Zorzi's apparent originality mostly consists in editorial selection and a quietist theological sensibility, while his real kabbalistic competence — visible in the *tal/rugiada* passage — remains strategically understated in the Name-analysis section. The chain Pico → Reuchlin → Zorzi emerges as the primary lineage of Christian Kabbalah into which *De harmonia mundi* inscribes itself.
